metadata.summary: Clean Architecture: concentric dependency rings pointing inward so domain and use cases stay free of framework, DB, and UI concerns.

content.title: Clean Architecture (Uncle Bob)
content.summary: Dependencies point inward through concentric circles: Domain (Entities) at the center, then Application (Use Cases), then Interface Adapters, then Frameworks and Drivers at the outside. Inner circles know nothing about outer circles.

why.p: Robert C. Martin (2012) formalised the dependency rule: source-code dependencies must point inward toward high-level policy. This makes domain logic and use cases independently testable without a running database, web framework, or UI. Swapping PostgreSQL for SQLite, replacing Express with Fastify, or exposing a CLI alongside REST touches only the outermost ring.

LLMs understand and respect explicit layer boundaries, making Clean Architecture a natural fit for agentic development: agents generate domain entities and use-cases first, then adapters, and architecture tests verify the dependency direction mechanically.

list.item: Domains with non-trivial business rules where leaking persistence or UI concerns into core logic will create technical debt within a year.
Projects that must support multiple UI surfaces (REST API + CLI + background workers) sharing the same business logic.
Long-lived applications where the framework or database is expected to change at least once during the product lifetime.
Teams that need comprehensive unit-test coverage of business logic without spinning up infrastructure.
Systems with heavy regulatory or audit requirements where invariants and policies must be clearly locatable.
CRUD-only admin tools or internal scripts — the domain layer will be empty and layering adds overhead without benefit.
Prototypes or MVPs where V1 is intentionally throwaway — premature layering slows delivery.
Microservices where the entire domain fits in 200 lines — apply the pattern at the system boundary instead.
Languages or runtimes where dependency-injection cost is prohibitive (Python or JS without DI containers can simulate it, but the boilerplate is real).
Domain Layer (Entities) — pure business objects and repository interfaces. No framework dependencies, no ORM, no logging, no clock. Inject everything via ports.
Application Layer (Use Cases) — orchestrates domain objects to fulfill business operations. Depends only on Domain. Defines DTO contracts and application-level interfaces (IEmailService, etc.).
Interface Adapters (Controllers, Presenters, Gateways) — translates between the outside world and the application layer. Contains repository implementations, web controllers, and serialisers.
Frameworks and Drivers (DB, Web, UI) — outermost ring: Express/Django/FastAPI, PostgreSQL ORM, React, etc. Nothing in here bleeds inward.

rule.statement: Every import MUST point inward: Domain imports nothing; Application imports only Domain; Infrastructure imports Application and Domain; Presentation imports Application and Domain but NOT Infrastructure.
rule.rationale: Enforcing this mechanically (via import-linter, dependency-cruiser, ArchUnit) prevents the well-known "service calls ORM directly" drift that erases the architecture over time.

example.title: Dependency table
example.code: Layer | Can depend on | Cannot depend on ---------------|------------------------|---------------------------------- Domain | nothing external | Application, Infrastructure, Presentation Application | Domain | Infrastructure, Presentation Infrastructure | Application, Domain | Presentation Presentation | Application, Domain | Infrastructure

section.name: folder-structure
section.code: src/ ├── domain/ │ ├── entities/ # Business objects (User.ts, Order.ts) │ ├── value-objects/ # Immutable domain concepts (Money.ts, Email.ts) │ └── repositories/ # Interfaces only (IUserRepository.ts) │ ├── application/ │ ├── use-cases/ # Business operations (CreateUser.ts, PlaceOrder.ts) │ ├── dto/ # Data transfer objects (UserDTO.ts) │ └── interfaces/ # Port definitions (IEmailService.ts) │ ├── infrastructure/ │ ├── persistence/ # Repository implementations (PostgresUserRepository.ts) │ ├── services/ # External service adapters (SendGridEmailService.ts) │ └── config/ # Environment, DI container (container.ts) │ └── presentation/ ├── api/ # REST/GraphQL controllers (UserController.ts) ├── cli/ # Command-line interface └── web/ # Web UI (if server-rendered)

section.name: entity-pattern
section.p: Domain entities must be rich, not anemic. Behaviour belongs on the entity; services exist only for cross-entity logic that has no natural home.

rule.statement: Entities MUST encapsulate their own invariants, raise domain events, and expose behaviour methods. Static factory methods (create, reconstitute) replace public constructors so construction always goes through invariant checks.
rule.rationale: The Anaemic Domain Model anti-pattern puts all logic in services and reduces entities to getters/setters. This defeats the purpose of Clean Architecture: there is no domain to protect.

example.title: Rich domain entity (TypeScript)
Use case (Application layer)
Anaemic entity — logic in service instead of entity
example.note: Logic in services that mutates entity state bypasses invariant checks and makes the domain untestable in isolation.

section.name: testing-strategy
section.p: Layer-by-layer testing approach for Clean Architecture:

list.item: Domain — unit tests with zero mocks. Pure functions, no setup required.
Application — unit tests mocking repositories and external service interfaces. Fast, no I/O.
Infrastructure — integration tests against real databases (testcontainers) or real external services in isolation.
Presentation — integration tests against the full application stack assembled in-memory, or E2E with a running server.

section.name: architecture-enforcement
section.p: Architecture rules decay silently if not enforced automatically. Run a linter on every PR.

example.title: import-linter config (Python)
example.code:
# .importlinter [importlinter] root_package = app
[importlinter:contract:layers] name = Clean Architecture layers type = layers layers = app.presentation app.infrastructure app.application app.domain
[importlinter:contract:domain-isolated] name = Domain has no outward deps type = forbidden source_modules = app.domain forbidden_modules = app.application app.infrastructure app.presentation django sqlalchemy fastapi
example.note: Run `lint-imports` in CI; fail the build on violation.

antipattern.description: Putting business logic in controllers (fat controller).
antipattern.reason: Controllers are in the Presentation ring; business logic belongs in Use Cases or Entities so it can be reused by CLI, queue workers, and scheduled jobs without HTTP overhead.

antipattern.description: Importing ORM models directly from the domain layer.
antipattern.reason: ORMs are an infrastructure detail. Domain must depend on nothing application-level — no logging, no ORM, no clock. Inject everything via ports.

antipattern.description: Exposing domain entities via the API (JSON-serialising them directly).
antipattern.reason: Domain entities change on business rules; API contracts change on consumer needs. Explicit DTOs at adapter boundaries prevent API breakage when the domain evolves.

antipattern.description: Skipping repository interfaces and calling the ORM directly from use cases.
antipattern.reason: Use cases become untestable without a real database, and swapping persistence requires touching business logic.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Recommended multi-agent workflow for implementing Clean Architecture:

list.item: Domain-modeler agent: analyzes requirements, drafts entities, value objects, and aggregate boundaries with ubiquitous-language glossary.
Scaffold agent: generates the layer structure, repository interfaces per aggregate, and port interfaces per use case.
Implementation agent (faion-feature-executor): implements one vertical slice (use case + adapters + tests) at a time.
Audit agent (faion-improver): periodic dependency-direction sweep and anaemic-model check.

section.prompt: INPUT: requirements <doc> and domain glossary. TASK: Identify aggregates, entities, value objects. For each aggregate output: - invariants (must always hold) - commands (state-changing operations) - queries (read-only, may bypass aggregate) - repository port signature Reject any "Manager"/"Service"/"Helper" naming; force domain-specific names.

ROLE: dependency-direction critic TASK: Run an architecture test that fails if domain/ imports infrastructure/ or presentation/. Output the violations with line numbers and propose the minimal refactor (move method, extract interface, invert).

section.name: pattern-selection

list.item: Domain has business logic worth protecting (not pure CRUD).
You might change the UI framework or database in future.
You need comprehensive unit testing without infrastructure.
Team understands SOLID principles.

section.p: Escalate to Hexagonal when multiple input channels are required. Add DDD tactical patterns when the domain becomes very complex with multiple subdomains.

checklist.check: Domain layer has zero imports from Application, Infrastructure, or Presentation.
All external dependencies (DB, cache, email, clock) are accessed via interface ports.
Repository interfaces live in Domain; implementations live in Infrastructure.
Use cases return DTOs, not raw domain entities.
Controllers are thin — no business logic, only HTTP translation.
Architecture test (import-linter / dependency-cruiser / ArchUnit) runs in CI.
Domain and Application layers are covered by fast unit tests (no DB required).
